title: SEGMENT 1 — The shortage, the stakes, and the one sentence I want you to leave with

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — The shortage, the stakes, and the one sentence I want you to leave with
Alright.
Good morning, everyone.
For the next two hours we're going to talk about xenotransplantation — the science of putting an organ from another species, in practice almost always a pig, into a human being and keeping it alive.
I'm warning you up front: this is one of those topics where the science is so fresh that some of what I'm telling you happened in the last calendar year.
We're going to talk about human beings, named human beings, who are alive right now with a pig's kidney filtering their blood.
That's the world we live in.
So buckle in.
Here is the one sentence I want you to walk out of here with, and then I'll spend two hours unpacking it.
Xenotransplantation is borrowing an organ from a pig — and CRISPR is what finally let us do it.
That's the whole thing.
Pig organ, human body, with the immune problem solved by genome editing.

Every other thing I'm going to tell you today — the alpha-gal sugar, the triple knockout, the porcine retroviruses, David Bennett's heart, Towana Looney's kidney, the FDA pathway, the gnotobiotic herds, the religious considerations, the 3D-printing rivals — every one of those is a variation on or an extension of that one core idea.
A pig grew an organ, we made the pig less foreign with editing, we transplanted the organ, and a human lived.
I want you to feel why this matters, and the way to feel it is with the numbers.
In the United States, right now, there are roughly one hundred thousand people on the organ transplant waiting list.
The list is not abstract.

It is a literal database run by an organization called UNOS — United Network for Organ Sharing — and each row of the database is a person who needs a heart, a liver, a kidney, a lung, or some combination, and who will die without one.
About seventeen of them die every day waiting.
Do the math.
That's more than six thousand people a year, in the United States alone, who are essentially executed by an organ shortage.
Most of them are on the kidney list.
The kidney list is the longest, and the wait for a kidney can stretch to five, six, seven years, during which the patient is tethered to a dialysis machine three days a week.
Dialysis keeps you alive, but it's not living.
Five years on dialysis is a death sentence in slow motion.
And this is the supply side.
There just are not enough human donors.
Brain-dead trauma victims, the traditional source, are a finite resource — and modern medicine, thankfully, has gotten very good at keeping head-injury patients alive.
Living donor kidneys help with the kidney list, but most people don't have a sibling who's a match and willing.
The math does not work.
The waiting list grows faster than the donor pool.
You cannot fix this with more organ donor checkboxes on driver's licenses.
So here's the dream, and the dream is at least a century old.
What if we could just manufacture organs?
What if every patient on the list could walk into a hospital and, within a week, get a custom-sized, immunologically compatible heart, or kidney, or lung?
What if the supply was, in principle, infinite?
For about a hundred years, doctors looked across the species barrier — at chimpanzees, at baboons, at sheep, at pigs — and asked: could we use their organs?
The very first kidney transplant in human medical history, in 1906, was a xenotransplant.
Not human-to-human.
A French surgeon named Mathieu Jaboulay sewed a pig kidney into a woman's elbow.
It lasted three days before it turned black and died.
Through the twentieth century, dozens more attempts followed, each one a disaster.
The most famous is Baby Fae, in 1984, who received a baboon heart and lived about three weeks.
The field was repeatedly humiliated.
By the 1990s, the consensus in transplantation was that xenotransplantation was a beautiful idea that the immune system would never permit.
And then, between roughly 2010 and 2022, the field came back from the dead.
The reason it came back is exactly what we talked about in the CRISPR lecture.

Once you can edit the genome of a pig — once you can knock out the genes that make a pig's organs look foreign to a human, and knock in the human genes that calm the human immune system down — the wall starts to crack.
By 2022, we had a man living with a 10-gene-edited pig heart.
By 2024, we had three patients in a row, each one a different person, receiving 69-edit pig kidneys.
The field went from impossible to clinical in about a decade.
That's why we're spending two hours on it.
So here's what I want from you in two hours.

If you walk out of here and can do three things — first, explain, to a confused friend, why the alpha-gal sugar is the central villain of the last fifty years of xeno research; second, walk through what the modern multi-gene-edited pig actually has done to it, gene by gene; and third, tell me the story of David Bennett and the 2024 kidney wave — Slayman, Pisano, Looney — and what the field learned from each — then we've succeeded.
Everything else is decoration on those three.
One last framing before we dive in.
Xenotransplantation is really two stories braided together.
One story is immunology — fifty years of fighting the human immune system's instant, scorched-earth response to anything non-human.
The other story is technology — three decades of cloning, transgenics, and finally CRISPR, which gave us the toolkit to remake a pig genome at will.
The first story explains why we needed the second.
You can't really understand why we edit pigs the way we do without understanding what the immune system does to an unedited pig organ.
So that's where we'll go.
But first, a stop on the supply side — why pigs, of all animals.
